title: Energy Is the Real Bottleneck: Why Huang Isn't Worried About Fabs but Fears Power Policy

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
When pressed on whether Nvidia can sustain its pace of doubling revenue year over year given upstream constraints—logic capacity, CoWoS packaging, HBM memory, EUV machines—Jensen Huang offers a surprisingly relaxed assessment. Every one of these bottlenecks, he claims, is a two-to-three year problem that resolves once a clear demand signal exists. The real constraint lies downstream: energy policy.

Huang points to CoWoS as a case study. Two years ago, it was the most discussed bottleneck in AI infrastructure. Nvidia and its partners swarmed the problem, doubling capacity repeatedly until TSMC now scales packaging at the same rate as logic. The same pattern, he argues, applies to HBM and even EUV machines from ASML. Once you can build one, you can build ten, and once you can build ten, you can build a million. Manufacturing bottlenecks yield to demand signals and capital.

Energy is fundamentally different. You cannot reindustrialize the United States—building chip fabs, AI factories, EV manufacturing, and robotics facilities—without massive new energy capacity. Unlike semiconductor supply chains where Huang can personally convince CEOs to invest, energy infrastructure involves regulatory frameworks, permitting timelines, and policy decisions that operate on longer horizons and involve different stakeholders entirely.

This distinction reshapes how one should think about AI scaling limitations. The narrative around AI compute scarcity often focuses on chip supply, but Huang redirects attention to the watt-level constraint. It's why Nvidia obsesses over performance per watt—in a power-constrained world, the company that delivers the most tokens per watt captures the most value from every gigawatt data center built.

The implication for policymakers is pointed: if you want AI leadership, the lever isn't just export controls or fab subsidies. It's ensuring that the energy infrastructure exists to power the industry at the scale it demands. Without that, all the chip capacity in the world sits idle.
